_meta.authoringNotes: ICD-10 codes J45.1 (predominantly allergic asthma with exercise) and J45.5 (exercise-induced bronchoconstriction). Content reviewed against GINA 2024, ATS/ERS EIB guideline, and clinical literature. Diagnostic thresholds and treatment recommendations cross-verified against UpToDate Lexicomp.

h1Title: Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction refers to transient airway narrowing that occurs during or within 5-20 minutes after vigorous physical activity, triggered by the rapid cooling and drying of the airway surface from high-volume breathing of cold, dry air. The condition affects up to 90 percent of elite athletes with asthma and 10 to 15 percent of the general population without underlying asthma. Key triggers include running in cold weather, cycling, swimming in chlorinated pools, and ice sports. The condition is diagnosed by an eucapnic voluntary hyperpnea challenge test or an exercise challenge — spirometry at rest is typically normal. With pre-exercise short-acting beta-agonist prophylaxis, approximately 80 percent of athletes with EIB can compete at a high level symptom-free.

keyStats.prevalence: 10-15% of general population; up to 90% of elite athletes with asthma
keyStats.demographics: Affects both children and adults equally; higher prevalence in endurance sport athletes
keyStats.avgAge: Onset most common in adolescence and young adulthood; can develop at any age
keyStats.globalCases: ~300 million people worldwide have asthma; EIB is the most common asthma phenotype in athletes

definition: Exercise-induced asthma (EIA), more precisely termed exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) in clinical nomenclature (ICD-10: J45.2), is the transient narrowing of the airways that occurs during or, more characteristically, within 5-30 minutes after physical exertion in people with hyperreactive airways. Unlike asthma triggered by allergens or infections, EIB is precipitated exclusively or predominantly by physical activity, with symptoms ranging from mild cough and shortness of breath to frank wheezing and respiratory distress. It is not a distinct disease from asthma — it represents the exercise-triggered expression of underlying bronchial hyperresponsiveness. EIB affects an estimated 5-20% of the general population, but up to 80-90% of people with a known asthma diagnosis report exercise-triggered symptoms. It is particularly prevalent in elite athletes, cold-weather sport participants, and children and adolescents. The pathophysiology involves water and heat loss from the airway surface during exercise, causing transient hyperosmolarity of the periciliary fluid, mast cell degranulation, and release of bronchoconstrictor mediators including histamine, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins. This is distinct from the eosinophilic allergic inflammation that characterizes atopic asthma, though both mechanisms often coexist. Diagnosis relies on spirometry demonstrating a 10% or greater fall in FEV1 from baseline following a standardized exercise challenge test or eucapnic voluntary hyperpnea (EVH) challenge, which is the gold standard for athletes. Management centers on pre-exercise short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) inhalation, warm-up protocols, and, for frequent symptoms, daily leukotriene receptor antagonist (LTRA) therapy. With proper diagnosis and management, virtually all people with EIB can exercise fully and compete at the highest level.

typesClassification.description: EIB occurs only with exercise and symptoms are absent at rest. Baseline spirometry is normal and there is no chronic asthma between exercise episodes. This variant is more common in non-atopic individuals and is entirely exercise-specific.

Exercise triggers symptoms in a patient with established allergic or non-allergic asthma. Baseline spirometry may show obstruction, and symptoms occur both with exercise and with other asthma triggers (allergens, cold air, viral infection). Treatment targets both the underlying asthma and exercise-specific management.

typesClassification.type: EIB with rhinitis and/or vocal cord dysfunction
typesClassification.description: Exercise symptoms overlap with or are amplified by upper airway dysfunction — postnasal drip, laryngeal irritation, or vocal cord adduction during exercise. Differentiating pure EIB from these mimics requires direct laryngoscopy and a exercise challenge with continuous laryngoscopy.

primarySymptoms: Shortness of breath beginning during or within 5-20 minutes of Vigorous exercise, with symptom peak at 5-10 minutes post-exercise.
Wheezing — a high-pitched musical sound on exhalation, audible without a stethoscope in moderate-severe episodes.
Chest tightness described as a pressure or constriction — often mistaken for a cardiac event by young athletes.
Cough that is dry and hacking at first, potentially productive of clear or white sputum after 15-30 minutes.
Fatigue disproportionate to the exercise intensity — athletes describe 'hitting a wall' earlier than usual.
Reduced exercise performance — slower times, earlier need to stop, inability to maintain previous pace.
Symptoms that resolve spontaneously within 30-90 minutes after exercise cessation in mild cases.
Nocturnal symptoms may follow a severe exercise episode, disrupting sleep for 1-2 nights.
Chest discomfort on deep inspiration — pleuritic-type pain that worsens with breathing in, distinct from cardiac pain.
Phlegm production after exercise, typically clear or white, not purulent (purulent suggests infection).
earlyWarningSigns: Coughing or mild chest tightness after a routine warm-up run — an early signal of airway hyperreactivity.
Reduced performance in cold-weather training sessions — cold, dry air is a potent EIB trigger.
A sensation of 'stuffy' or restricted airways during the first 5 minutes of high-intensity interval training.
Exercise-induced fatigue that is disproportionate to the training load or new to the athlete.
A history of atopic disease (allergic rhinitis, eczema) or known asthma in the athlete increases pre-test probability.
emergencySigns: Severe dyspnea with audible stridor (high-pitched inspiratory sound) — suggests upper airway obstruction from anaphylaxis or vocal cord dysfunction, not simple EIB.
Failure to improve within 15-20 minutes of inhaled short-acting beta-agonist — indicates severe bronchospasm requiring escalation.
Cyanosis (bluish lips or fingertips) — a sign of significant hypoxemia requiring emergency care.
Retractions (use of accessory muscles, intercostal pulling) at rest — indicates respiratory muscle fatigue.
Severe chest pain, fainting, or loss of consciousness — exclude cardiac causes before attributing to EIB.

causes.description: The primary pathophysiologic mechanism: rapid breathing during exercise draws large volumes of cold, dry air across the airway mucosa. The resulting thermal gradient and osmotic stress cause epithelial cell damage and release of inflammatory mediators — leukotrienes, prostaglandins, and histamine — that contract bronchial smooth muscle.

Evaporation of airway surface liquid during exercise creates a hyperosmolar environment in the bronchial lumen. Inflammatory cells and epithelial cells sense this change and degranulate, releasing bronchoconstrictor mediators.

The cooling of airway tissue during exercise causes vasoconstriction; rapid rewarming on stopping exercise leads to reactive vasodilation and plasma extravasation, contributing to airway edema and narrowing.

In patients with underlying asthma, the baseline chronic airway inflammation primes the airways for a larger response to the exercise-triggered mediator release, producing more severe bronchoconstriction.
Cold air, low humidity, air pollution (ozone, particulate matter), and chloramine exposure in indoor swimming pools are potent co-factors that lower the threshold for EIB symptoms.

High-intensity exercise generates the greatest minute ventilation and therefore the greatest heat/water loss. Running, cycling, and cross-country skiing are the highest-risk activities; swimming is relatively protective due to the warm, humidified air above the water.

riskFactors.description: Distance running, cycling, swimming, and winter sports carry the highest EIB prevalence — up to 50-90% in competitive swimmers and winter sport athletes.
Asthma is the single strongest predictor of EIB. Approximately 40-50% of all asthma patients experience exercise-induced symptoms.

riskFactors.factor: Atopic history (allergic rhinitis, eczema, food allergy)
riskFactors.description: Atopic individuals have higher baseline airway reactivity and are more likely to develop EIB, particularly the pure allergic type.
Outdoor training in sub-freezing temperatures dramatically increases EIB prevalence — halving the air temperature roughly doubles the risk.
Sustained exercise above 90% of maximum heart rate produces the highest minute ventilation and therefore the greatest airway stress.
Swimming pool chlorine byproducts (chloramines) are potent airway irritants that both trigger and sensitize the airways. Competitive swimmers have some of the highest EIB rates of any sport.
High ozone days lower the exercise threshold for bronchoconstriction and increase the severity of EIB episodes.
A recent URI increases airway hyperreactivity for 2-6 weeks after the illness, temporarily worsening EIB severity.
Athletes with inadequate maintenance inhaled corticosteroid therapy experience more severe and more frequent EIB episodes than those whose asthma is well-controlled.
Return to high-intensity training in early season before full aerobic conditioning increases the magnitude of ventilatory stress and EIB likelihood.

affectedDemographics: Elite athletes: 20-50% of Olympic-level athletes report exercise-related respiratory symptoms; objective testing confirms EIB in 8-12% of the general population
Swimmers: Highest prevalence of any sport group — up to 80% of elite swimmers report symptoms, partly due to chlorine byproducts
Winter sport athletes: 25-50% prevalence in cross-country skiers, speed skaters, and ice hockey players due to cold air exposure
Children and adolescents: 10-20% of school-age children have exercise-related respiratory symptoms; EIB is the most common cause of exercise-induced dyspnea in this group
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of EIB requires objective confirmation because self-reported exercise symptoms have poor specificity — up to 50% of patients with exercise-related breathing complaints do not have EIB on challenge testing, while many patients with confirmed EIB do not report symptoms. The gold standard is the eucapnic voluntary hyperpnea (EVH) challenge: the patient breathes a dry gas mixture containing approximately 4.5% CO2 and 21% O2 at 60-85% of maximum voluntary ventilation for 6 minutes. A 10% or greater fall in FEV1 from baseline at 3, 5, 10, 15, and 30 minutes post-challenge confirms EIB. The exercise challenge test is a valid alternative: the patient runs on a treadmill or cycles on an ergometer at 80-90% of predicted maximum heart rate until symptoms develop or 6-8 minutes have elapsed. A 10% fall in FEV1 within 30 minutes of stopping exercise is diagnostic. Spirometry at rest is typically normal in pure EIB and is not sufficient alone for diagnosis. Methacholine challenge is an alternative when EVH or exercise testing is unavailable — a provocative concentration causing 20% fall (PC20) below 4 mg/mL indicates airway hyperresponsiveness but is less specific for EIB and yields more false positives.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard diagnostic test for EIB. The patient breathes a dry gas mixture at high ventilation for 6 minutes. A 10% or greater fall in FEV1 at any post-challenge time point confirms EIB.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Aspirometry baseline is established, then a nose clip is applied and the patient breathes the gas mixture through a mouthpiece for 6 minutes. Spirometry is repeated at intervals for 30 minutes. The test takes approximately 45 minutes total.

diagnosticTests.test: Exercise challenge test (treadmill or cycle ergometer)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Physiologic test replicating real-world conditions. Used when EVH is unavailable or to assess athletes in their sport-specific environment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Baseline spirometry is recorded; the athlete then exercises at 80-90% of maximum predicted heart rate for 6-8 minutes in an environment set to the relevant temperature/humidity. Spirometry is measured at intervals after exercise.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes baseline lung function and degree of reversibility. In pure EIB, baseline spirometry is normal and bronchodilator response is absent at rest.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard spirometry test performed before and 15 minutes after inhaling 400 mcg of salbutamol. A greater than 12% and 200 mL improvement in FEV1 indicates reversible obstruction, seen in asthma but not pure EIB.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assess airway hyperresponsiveness when EVH or exercise testing is unavailable. Less specific for EIB than EVH — positive results require clinical correlation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry is performed after inhaling increasing concentrations of methacholine (a bronchoconstrictor agent). The test stops when FEV1 falls by 20% or the maximum dose is reached.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Marker of type-2 airway inflammation. Elevated FeNO supports underlying asthma as the diagnosis; normal FeNO favors pure EIB.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A breath test — the patient exhales at a constant flow rate into a device for 10 seconds. Results are available immediately; levels above 25 ppb in non-smokers suggest eosinophilic airway inflammation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Home monitoring tool to identify exercise-related PEF drops. Less reliable than formal challenge testing but useful for monitoring response to therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient measures PEF with a handheld meter before and after exercise over a 2-week period, recording results in a diary. A 15% or greater drop after exercise is suggestive but not definitive.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of EIB follows a two-tier approach: acute symptom management and preventive therapy. For acute symptoms, a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) inhaler — salbutamol (albuterol) 100-200 mcg via metered-dose inhaler — provides bronchodilation within 5 minutes, with peak effect at 15-30 minutes. The SABA dose may be repeated once after 15 minutes if symptoms persist. For prevention, the first-line strategy is a SABA taken 15 minutes before exercise, which prevents symptoms in approximately 80% of patients. If SABA pre-treatment is insufficient (more than twice weekly), or if the athlete also has underlying asthma, a daily inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) is added — budesonide or fluticasone at low-to-medium dose, which reduces airway inflammation and hyperresponsiveness. Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast 10 mg daily) are an alternative or adjunct when ICS is not preferred or insufficient. A leukotriene antagonist taken 1-2 hours before exercise is effective for prevention in approximately 70% of patients and is particularly useful when the exercise schedule is unpredictable. Long-acting beta-agonists (LABA) such as formoterol have a role as exercise pre-treatment when combined with an ICS, but LABA monotherapy carries a black box warning for increased asthma mortality and must not be used alone. Athletes competing under WADA regulations should verify that all medications are permitted — SABAs are allowed with a declaration; systemic corticosteroids require a therapeutic use exemption.

medicalTreatments.name: Salbutamol/albuterol inhaler (100-200 mcg as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-acting beta-agonist — first-line for acute symptom relief and pre-exercise prophylaxis. Works within 5 minutes, peaks at 15-30 minutes. Used 15 minutes before exercise or at symptom onset. No tolerance with standard dosing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pre-exercise prophylaxis prevents symptoms in approximately 80% of patients with pure EIB.

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroids (budesonide 200-400 mcg/day; fluticasone 100-250 mcg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line daily maintenance therapy for athletes with EIB and underlying asthma. Reduces airway inflammation and hyperresponsiveness over 2-4 weeks. Not for acute symptom relief — onset of action is 1-2 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces EIB frequency by 50-70% in patients with underlying asthma; minimal benefit in pure EIB without inflammation.

medicalTreatments.name: Montelukast 10 mg daily (or 5 mg for children 6-14 years)
medicalTreatments.description: Leukotriene receptor antagonist — blocks the bronchoconstrictor effect of cysteinyl leukotrienes. Can be used daily for maintenance or 1-2 hours before exercise for on-demand prevention. Well-tolerated in children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pre-exercise prevention in approximately 70% of patients; less effective than SABA pre-treatment in head-to-head trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Beclomethasone/formoterol combination inhaler (daily maintenance plus as-needed)
medicalTreatments.description: ICS/LABA combination used for athletes who have both underlying asthma and exercise symptoms not controlled by SABA pre-treatment alone. Provides both maintenance anti-inflammatory effect and rescue bronchodilation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Superior to ICS alone for exercise symptom control; equivalent to SABA pre-treatment for most athletes.

medicalTreatments.name: Cromolyn sodium (Intal) inhaler
medicalTreatments.description: Mast cell stabilizer — prevents degranulation and mediator release. Used before exercise as prophylaxis in patients who prefer non-steroidal therapy or cannot tolerate beta-agonists. Requires dosing 10-15 minutes before exercise.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate efficacy — approximately 60% reduction in exercise symptoms; less effective than SABAs or leukotriene antagonists.

medicalTreatments.name: Ipratropium bromide inhaler (40-80 mcg as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: Anticholinergic bronchodilator — blocks muscarinic receptors in bronchial smooth muscle. Used as adjunctive therapy when SABAs are insufficient or not tolerated. Onset 15-30 minutes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adds bronchodilation when combined with SABAs in moderate-severe EIB; alone it is less effective than SABA monotherapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Not applicable
surgicalOptions.description: EIB is managed medically with pharmacotherapy. Surgical intervention does not play a role in EIB management. Athletes with severe refractory EIB despite optimal medical therapy should be re-evaluated for alternative diagnoses (vocal cord dysfunction, dysfunctional breathing, cardiac causes) rather than referred for surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nasal breathing and breathing technique training
alternativeTreatments.description: Nasal breathing during exercise warms and humidifies incoming air, reducing the heat/water loss that triggers EIB. Breathing pattern retraining (Papworth method) has shown benefit in reducing exercise-related symptoms in athletes with dysfunctional breathing patterns, which often coexist with EIB.

alternativeTreatments.name: Warm-up interval training
alternativeTreatments.description: A graduated warm-up — 10-15 minutes of progressively increasing intensity — can induce a refractory period in some patients, where subsequent high-intensity exercise produces fewer symptoms. The mechanism is thought to involve depletion of available mediator stores in inflammatory cells.

whySeeSpecialist: A pulmonologist or sports medicine physician should be involved when an athlete has exercise-related breathing symptoms that impair performance, when over-the-counter SABA pre-treatment is insufficient, when the diagnosis is uncertain (symptoms suggest EIB but challenge testing is negative), or when the athlete requires formal documentation for sport eligibility or anti-doping purposes. A sports cardiologist may be needed when cardiac causes cannot be excluded.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a pulmonologist with sports medicine training or a sports medicine physician with respiratory expertise. The ability to perform and interpret eucapnic voluntary hyperpnea or exercise challenge testing is the key competency. Ask about familiarity with WADA regulations if the athlete competes internationally.
preventionStrategies: Use a short-acting beta-agonist inhaler 15 minutes before exercise as primary prophylaxis — this single step prevents symptoms in approximately 80% of patients
Perform a graduated warm-up of 10-15 minutes at progressive intensity before main exercise — this induces a refractory period that reduces symptoms during subsequent high-intensity effort
Breathe through the nose during exercise when possible — nasal breathing warms, humidifies, and filters inspired air, reducing the respiratory heat and water loss that triggers EIB
Choose indoor venues with warm, humidified air over outdoor cold-air training when EIB is severe — swimming in a heated pool is relatively protective
Time intense outdoor training to mid-day in winter rather than early morning or evening when temperatures are lowest
lifestyleModifications: Maintain optimal asthma control with daily inhaled corticosteroid therapy if underlying asthma is present — well-controlled asthma dramatically reduces EIB severity
Avoid training during high air pollution or ozone alert days — pollutant exposure lowers the exercise intensity threshold for bronchoconstriction
Shower and change clothing immediately after swimming in chlorinated pools — chloramine residue on skin and clothing continues to irritate airways
Carry a short-acting beta-agonist inhaler during all exercise sessions and ensure coaching staff are trained to recognize and respond to severe symptoms
Hydrate adequately before and during exercise — dehydration concentrates airway irritants and may worsen symptoms
Track symptoms in a training diary to identify patterns (weather, intensity, specific activities) and share with the treating clinician

recommended: High-antioxidant foods — colorful fruits and vegetables (berries, citrus, leafy greens) support airway tissue health
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) — modestly anti-inflammatory and associated with reduced airway reactivity in some studies
Vitamin D from fortified foods or safe sun exposure — deficiency is associated with worse asthma control and more EIB episodes
Adequate water intake — supports airway surface liquid and mucociliary clearance
Anti-inflammatory foods — turmeric, ginger, and green tea contain compounds that may modestly reduce airway inflammation
avoid: Sulfite-containing preserved foods (dried fruits, processed meats, wine) — sulfites can trigger bronchoconstriction in sensitive individuals
High-fat, low-fiber processed foods — associated with higher systemic inflammation and worse asthma control
Refined sugar and high-glycemic foods — may increase systemic inflammation in some athletes
Beer and wine in large quantities — alcohol and its metabolites can worsen airway reactivity
Very cold beverages consumed immediately before intense exercise — thermal shock to the oropharynx may exacerbate symptoms

exerciseGuidelines: All forms of exercise are appropriate for patients with well-controlled EIB. Swimming is particularly well-tolerated due to the warm, humid air environment. Cold-weather activities (cross-country skiing, ice hockey) require more careful pre-treatment. Interval training allows recovery periods that reduce cumulative ventilatory stress. Competitive athletes with EIB should work with a sports physician to optimize prophylaxis and obtain necessary documentation for sport governing bodies.
dailyManagement: Use SABA inhaler 15 minutes before planned exercise or physical education class
Carry the inhaler at all times — symptoms can develop unexpectedly with unanticipated activity
Take maintenance ICS daily if prescribed — consistency is the key to reducing baseline hyperreactivity
Measure peak flow before and after exercise if using home monitoring
Note weather conditions, activity type, and symptoms in a training diary to share with the clinician
prognosis: With appropriate pharmacologic management, the prognosis for EIB is excellent. Approximately 80% of athletes with EIB achieve complete symptom control with SABA pre-treatment and adequate warm-up — the vast majority compete at the highest levels, including Olympic athletes. For patients with EIB and underlying asthma, maintenance ICS therapy reduces the frequency and severity of exercise episodes and improves overall quality of life. EIB does not cause permanent lung damage — unlike chronic asthma, pure EIB does not lead to fixed airflow obstruction. However, severe uncontrolled EIB impairing training can lead to deconditioning and reduced aerobic capacity, which resolves once symptoms are well-controlled. Undiagnosed or undertreated EIB is associated with exercise avoidance, reduced physical activity, and worse quality of life in children and adolescents. The long-term outlook is determined more by adequate treatment access and adherence than by the severity of the condition itself.
recoveryTimeline: With appropriate pre-treatment, recovery is immediate — symptoms either do not develop or are mild and short-lived. After an untreated exercise episode, symptoms typically peak at 5-15 minutes post-exercise and resolve over 30-90 minutes with rest. Inhaled SABA accelerates recovery, reducing symptoms to minimal or zero within 15 minutes. An athlete who has an acute EIB episode should wait until fully asymptomatic and bronchodilated before resuming exercise — typically 2-4 hours after a dose of reliever medication.
complications: Chronic undertreatment leading to exercise avoidance and sedentary lifestyle — particularly harmful in children and adolescents whose physical development depends on regular activity
Reduced athletic performance and competitive ranking due to unprophylaxed exercise symptoms
Exacerbation of underlying asthma if maintenance therapy is not optimized — uncontrolled asthma leads to more severe and more frequent exercise-induced episodes
Severe bronchospasm requiring emergency department visit or hospitalization — typically occurs when pre-treatment is omitted or when symptoms are underestimated
Misdiagnosis of cardiac disease — exertional chest tightness in young athletes is often assumed to be EIB when it warrants cardiac evaluation

supportResources.name: GINA — Global Initiative for Asthma
supportResources.description: Internationally recognized asthma guidelines including the EIB chapter; free PDF download.

supportResources.name: American College of Sports Medicine — Exercise is Medicine
supportResources.description: Resources on exercise prescription for patients with chronic respiratory conditions.

supportResources.name: WADA — Prohibited List (inhaler permissions)
supportResources.description: Current anti-doping regulations for asthma and EIB medications in competitive athletes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma involves chronic airway inflammation and reversible obstruction at rest — spirometry shows obstruction even outside exercise. EIB without underlying asthma has normal spirometry at rest and symptoms only with exercise.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vocal Cord Dysfunction (VCD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: VCD causes inspiratory stridor and throat tightness that worsens with exercise, mimicking EIB. Laryngoscopy during an episode shows paradoxical vocal cord adduction during inspiration. Spirometry shows a truncated inspiratory loop rather than an obstructive expiratory pattern.

confusedWithConditions.name: Exercise-Induced Anaphylaxis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaphylaxis involves urticaria, angioedema, hypotension, and upper airway swelling — a distinct presentation from isolated bronchoconstriction. The two can coexist when food ingestion precedes exercise.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: EIB is the most common exercise-related phenotype of asthma and shares the same inflammatory pathway (eosinophilic/type-2 inflammation). Most athletes with EIB have some degree of underlying asthma.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Upper airway inflammation (allergic rhinitis) worsens the sensation of EIB symptoms by impairing nasal breathing and allowing more unconditioned air to reach the lower airways.

relatedConditions.name: Vocal Cord Dysfunction
relatedConditions.relevance: Coexists in 30-50% of patients referred for evaluation of exercise-related dyspnea. Often mistaken for refractory EIB; direct laryngoscopy confirms the diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: COPD
relatedConditions.relevance: Exercise intolerance in COPD involves a different pathophysiology (fixed airflow limitation, ventilation-perfusion mismatch) but can mimic EIB in older patients who smoke. Spirometry distinguishes the two.

faqs.question: Is exercise-induced asthma the same as asthma?
faqs.answer: EIB is a condition in which exercise triggers symptoms in someone whose airways are hyperreactive. In many patients, this hyperreactivity is part of underlying asthma — the exercise is just one trigger among many. In others, the hyperreactivity is specifically exercise-triggered and the patient has no symptoms at rest or with other triggers. Both groups benefit from treatment.

faqs.question: How is exercise-induced bronchoconstriction diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The gold-standard test is the eucapnic voluntary hyperpnea (EVH) challenge — breathing a dry gas mixture at high ventilation for 6 minutes. A 10% or greater drop in FEV1 after the test confirms EIB. An exercise challenge test (running or cycling until symptoms develop) is a valid alternative.

faqs.question: What is the best medication for EIB?
faqs.answer: A short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) inhaler such as salbutamol taken 15 minutes before exercise is the first-line treatment and prevention. If exercise symptoms occur more than twice weekly despite SABA pre-treatment, a daily inhaled corticosteroid is added to reduce the underlying airway hyperreactivity.

faqs.question: Can I use my asthma inhaler before exercise?
faqs.answer: Yes — a short-acting beta-agonist inhaler (salbutamol/albuterol) is the standard pre-exercise treatment for EIB. One to two puffs 15 minutes before exercise prevents symptoms in approximately 80% of patients.

faqs.question: Does EIB get worse with age?
faqs.answer: EIB itself does not worsen with age in a predictable way, but underlying asthma can progress over time. Many children with exercise-related symptoms have improvement as they mature. A physician should re-evaluate any change in symptom pattern.

faqs.question: Can I still be a competitive athlete with EIB?
faqs.answer: Yes — athletes with well-controlled EIB compete at the highest levels of sport. Most Olympic athletes with asthma or EIB use pre-treatment strategies that allow them to train and compete fully. Documentation requirements vary by sport governing body.

faqs.question: What triggers EIB besides exercise?
faqs.answer: Cold air, dry air, air pollution, ozone, and chloramine exposure in swimming pools are the main environmental co-factors. Viral infections also temporarily worsen airway hyperreactivity. Exercise intensity is the strongest trigger.

faqs.question: Is swimming good or bad for EIB?
faqs.answer: Swimming is one of the best-tolerated activities for most patients with EIB because the air above a heated pool is warm and humidified, reducing the heat and water loss that triggers bronchoconstriction. The exception is swimmers with chloramine sensitivity, in whom pool chemicals can cause symptoms.

faqs.question: Can diet affect EIB symptoms?
faqs.answer: Dietary factors have modest effects compared to pharmacotherapy. Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish may reduce airway inflammation, vitamin D deficiency should be corrected, and sulfite-containing foods should be avoided in sensitive individuals. Adequate hydration supports airway surface liquid.

faqs.question: Why does cold air trigger EIB?
faqs.answer: Cold air is dry as well as cold. Rapid breathing of cold, dry air across the airway mucosa causes thermal and osmotic stress to the epithelial cells, which triggers the release of inflammatory mediators (leukotrienes, histamine) that cause bronchoconstriction. The colder the air, the greater the stress.

faqs.question: Is EIB life-threatening?
faqs.answer: EIB is not typically life-threatening. Most episodes are self-limited and respond to a SABA inhaler within minutes. Life-threatening bronchospasm in EIB is rare and almost always occurs when pre-treatment is omitted and the episode is unrecognized or untreated.

faqs.question: How long do EIB symptoms last?
faqs.answer: Symptoms peak 5-15 minutes after exercise and typically resolve within 30-90 minutes without treatment. With a SABA inhaler, recovery is faster — within 15-30 minutes of taking the dose.

faqs.question: Can EIB be cured?
faqs.answer: EIB does not have a cure in the traditional sense, but it is highly controllable with the right treatment strategy. Maintenance inhaled corticosteroids reduce underlying hyperreactivity, and pre-exercise SABA prophylaxis prevents symptoms. Many athletes achieve complete symptom control.

faqs.question: Does a warm-up help prevent EIB?
faqs.answer: Yes — a graduated warm-up of 10-15 minutes at progressive intensity can induce a refractory period in many patients, reducing symptoms during subsequent high-intensity exercise. The effect lasts approximately 2-3 hours.

faqs.question: Can EIB cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: Pure EIB does not cause permanent lung damage or fixed airflow obstruction. Unlike chronic asthma, EIB specifically triggered by exercise does not lead to structural airway changes over time. The concern is more about quality of life and athletic performance than lung destruction.

faqs.question: Is it safe to exercise during an EIB episode?
faqs.answer: Stop exercise immediately when EIB symptoms develop. Wait until symptoms have fully resolved — after using a SABA inhaler — before resuming. Returning to exercise while still symptomatic risks a more severe episode.

faqs.question: Do athletes need a Therapeutic Use Exemption for EIB medications?
faqs.answer: Short-acting beta-agonists (salbutamol) are permitted by WADA with a declaration — no TUE is required. Inhaled corticosteroids require a declaration only. Oral corticosteroids require a TUE for competitive athletes. Athletes should verify current regulations with their national anti-doping authority.

faqs.question: Can mouth breathing during exercise worsen EIB?
faqs.answer: Yes — mouth breathing bypasses the nasal conditioning of inspired air, delivering cold, dry air directly to the lower airways. Nose breathing during exercise warms, humidifies, and filters the air, reducing the trigger for EIB. Conscious nose breathing is a useful non-pharmacologic strategy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between EIB and exercise-induced anaphylaxis?
faqs.answer: EIB causes bronchoconstriction — wheezing, coughing, and shortness of breath — with no systemic symptoms. Exercise-induced anaphylaxis is an allergic reaction that involves urticaria, angioedema, hypotension, and potentially fatal airway obstruction. The two are distinct conditions and are treated differently.

faqs.question: How is EIB different from vocal cord dysfunction?
faqs.answer: Vocal cord dysfunction causes upper airway obstruction — inspiratory stridor, throat tightness — rather than lower airway bronchoconstriction. Spirometry in VCD shows a truncated inspiratory loop (flattened inspiration curve); EIB shows obstructive expiratory pattern. Direct laryngoscopy confirms VCD.

metaDescription: Exercise-induced asthma causes wheezing and breathlessness during or after physical activity. Learn symptoms, triggers, treatment, and how to exercise safely.

images.altText: Blue metered-dose inhaler (SABA) used for pre-exercise prophylaxis and acute symptom relief in exercise-induced bronchoconstriction
images.caption: A short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) inhaler — the first-line treatment and prevention for exercise-induced bronchoconstriction.

images.altText: Spirometry trace showing normal flow-volume loop in exercise-induced bronchoconstriction at rest
images.caption: Spirometry at rest is typically normal in pure exercise-induced bronchoconstriction — challenge testing is required for diagnosis.
